faqs.question: How long does it take to form an LLC by yourself?
faqs.answer: The actual filing takes 30-60 minutes once you have all information ready. Processing time varies by state — online filings in many states are approved in 1-3 business days. Some states take 2-4 weeks.

faqs.question: What is the New York publication requirement?
faqs.answer: New York requires LLC owners to publish a notice of formation in two designated newspapers in the county of the LLC's address for six consecutive weeks. This can cost $500-$2,000+ depending on the county. New York City is the most expensive. Factor this into your cost comparison if you are forming in New York.

faqs.question: Can I change my formation from DIY to a service later?
faqs.answer: You cannot retroactively use a formation service for an already-formed LLC. But you can hire a registered agent service, get compliance reminders, and obtain an operating agreement through services like ZenBusiness after formation — just not the initial filing.

title: LLC Formation Checklist for Personal Errands & Concierge Services
metaTitle: Form an LLC for Your Errand or Concierge Business: A Step-by-Step Guide
metaDescription: Starting your Personal Errands or Concierge Service? This checklist guides TaskRabbit operators, senior companions, and errand runners through forming an LLC, securing licenses, and setting up your business correctly.
primaryKeyword: LLC for Personal Errands & Concierge Services
intro: Launching a Personal Errands & Concierge Service means you'll be helping clients with a wide range of tasks, from grocery shopping for seniors to managing busy professionals' to-do lists. Properly setting up your business, especially forming an LLC, isn't just one step—it's seven distinct actions, each building on the last. Many common business mistakes happen because new owners do these out of order or skip one entirely. This complete checklist ensures you set up your personal errand or concierge business in the right sequence, protecting yourself and your assets from day one.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: To successfully form an LLC for your personal errands or concierge service business, follow these seven steps in order: choose your entity type and state, check your chosen business name for availability, file your Articles of Organization, get your Employer Identification Number (EIN), open a dedicated business bank account, draft a solid Operating Agreement, and secure all required local, state, and specialized licenses and permits. Each step unlocks the next. Do not skip ahead.

sections.heading: Step 1: Choose Your Entity Type and State
sections.body: For most solo errand runners, personal shoppers, or independent senior companions, an LLC in your home state offers essential personal liability protection without the complexity of a corporation. This is critical in an industry where you're entering clients' homes, handling their property, or even transporting them. An LLC protects your personal assets (like your home and savings) if an incident occurs while you're on a client's errand—for example, accidentally damaging property or a mishap during a task. While you could start as a sole proprietorship, the minimal cost of an LLC is often a worthwhile investment for the peace of mind. Reference a comparison guide if you're weighing more complex options like a multi-member LLC for future partnerships. Time: 30-60 minutes of research. Cost: $0 to decide.

sections.heading: Step 2: Check Business Name Availability
sections.body: Your business name should be memorable and convey trust for a personal service. Before settling on names like 'Caring Hands Concierge LLC' or 'Reliable Errands Today,' run three key searches: 1) Your state's business name database (Secretary of State website) to ensure no other businesses have it. 2) The USPTO federal trademark database (tess.uspto.gov) to prevent future legal issues, especially if you plan to expand or franchise. 3) Domain registrar search (Namecheap or Google Domains) to secure your website address and social media handles. All three must come back clear to avoid headaches. Time: 30 minutes. Cost: $0.

sections.heading: Step 3: File Your Articles of Organization
sections.body: This is the official registration for your LLC with your state. You'll file through your Secretary of State's online portal or use a formation service. You'll need: your chosen business name (e.g., 'Helping Hands Errands & Concierge, LLC'), your registered agent's name and address (this is who receives legal mail), your name and address as a member, and a business purpose. For errand and concierge services, most states accept 'any lawful purpose,' but you could specify 'providing personal assistance, errand running, and senior companion services.' Time: 15-60 minutes to file, 1-3 weeks for state processing. Cost: $50-$500 in state fees + optional formation service fee.

sections.heading: Step 4: Get Your EIN
sections.body: An Employer Identification Number (EIN) is like a Social Security Number for your business. You'll need it even if you're a single-member LLC without employees, as it's required for opening a business bank account and filing business taxes. Apply for free at irs.gov. The process takes about five minutes and provides an instant EIN. Do not pay anyone to get this for you; it's a simple, free online application. Time: 5 minutes. Cost: $0. Available Monday-Friday 7am-10pm Eastern.

sections.heading: Step 5: Open a Business Bank Account
sections.body: Separating your personal and business finances is non-negotiable for an LLC, especially when you're handling client funds for purchases (e.g., groceries, shopping for gifts) or receiving direct payments for services. Bring your Articles of Organization, EIN letter, and government ID. Online banks (like Mercury, Relay, Novo) are often faster, free, and great for mobile service providers. Traditional banks might be better if you plan to handle significant cash for clients or build relationships for future business loans. Open this account before you accept any client payments or make any business purchases like dedicated scheduling software or fuel. Time: 20-30 minutes to apply, 1-3 days to open. Cost: $0 for most online banks.

sections.heading: Step 6: Draft Your Operating Agreement
sections.body: Even if you're a single-member LLC offering personal errands, an Operating Agreement is a vital internal document. It outlines your business's ownership, management, and operating procedures. For a solo venture, a quality template from your formation service or NOLO is usually sufficient. If you plan to partner with another errand runner or offer joint concierge services (multi-member LLC), hire an attorney to draft a robust agreement. Sign and date the agreement, store it with your Articles of Organization, and update it any time ownership or governance changes. Time: 30 minutes for a template, 1-2 weeks with an attorney. Cost: $0 (template) to $1,500+ (attorney).

sections.heading: Step 7: Get Required Licenses and Permits
sections.body:
This step is critical for personal errand and concierge services due to the varied nature of tasks and direct client interaction. Use the SBA license and permit tool, but expect to need more: 
*   **General Business License:** Almost all cities and counties require this ($25-$150/year). 
*   **Background Checks:** Essential if you're entering clients' homes, handling valuables, or working with vulnerable populations (seniors, children). Some states or cities may require fingerprinting or specific clearances. 
*   **Commercial Auto Insurance:** If you use your personal vehicle for significant business mileage, or especially if you transport clients (e.g., senior companion services), your personal auto policy likely won't cover you. You'll need a commercial policy. 
*   **General Liability Insurance:** Crucial for protecting against claims of property damage or injury that might occur while performing services in a client's home. 
*   **Bond Insurance:** Consider a 'fidelity bond' if you're handling client cash, credit cards, or making significant purchases on their behalf, offering peace of mind to clients. 
*   **Zoning Permits:** If you operate a home-based business, verify local zoning rules, especially if clients visit you (less common for mobile errand services). 
*   **Specialized Licenses:** Less common, but if you offer niche services like medical appointment transport, you might need specific transportation licenses or permits. Apply for everything before you accept your first client. Time: 2-4 hours of research, days to weeks for processing checks and permits. Cost: $25-$500+ depending on state, city, and required background checks/insurance policies.

affiliates.name: ZenBusiness
affiliates.description: Handles steps 3, 4, and 6 in one transaction

affiliates.name: Northwest Registered Agent
affiliates.description: Privacy-first formation with registered agent and operating agreement support

affiliates.name: Mercury
affiliates.description: Best business bank account for step 5

affiliates.name: SBA License and Permit Tool
affiliates.description: Free tool for identifying license requirements in step 7

faqs.question: How long does it take to form an LLC from start to finish?
faqs.answer: The filing itself takes a few hours spread across the steps. State processing for Articles of Organization takes 1-3 weeks in most states (some offer 24-hour expedited processing for an extra fee). Bank account opening adds 1-3 business days. Plan for 2-4 weeks from starting to having a fully operational business entity.

faqs.question: What order do I do these steps in — can I skip ahead?
faqs.answer: No. You must have your LLC formed before applying for an EIN. You need the EIN before opening a bank account. The operating agreement should reflect the entity as formed. Licenses and permits can sometimes be applied for in parallel with later steps, but most require your EIN.

faqs.question: What if my state has different requirements?
faqs.answer: The steps are consistent, but specifics vary. California requires an initial Statement of Information within 90 days. New York has a newspaper publication requirement. Some states require an initial report separate from the annual report. Your formation service or Secretary of State website will flag state-specific requirements.

title: Best Accounting Software for Personal Errands & Concierge Services
metaTitle: Wave, FreshBooks, QuickBooks, Xero: Best Accounting Software for Personal Errands & Concierge Services
metaDescription: Choosing accounting software for your errand running, personal shopping, or senior companion business? Compare Wave, FreshBooks, QuickBooks, and Xero to find the best fit for invoicing, expense tracking, and clean books without the high cost.
primaryKeyword: best accounting software for personal errands
intro: Many personal errand runners, concierge service providers, and senior companions pick accounting software without really thinking, then regret it. The right choice for your business depends on if you need easy invoicing for clients, simple expense tracking for mileage and supplies, or quick reports for tax time. And, how much you want to pay before your business earns steady income.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: Wave is the best default for new errand runners or senior companions needing free, basic bookkeeping. FreshBooks is ideal for personal shoppers or concierge services that send many invoices. QuickBooks Online is the safest choice if you plan to hire help or an accountant, as they all know it. Xero is rarely the best fit for this business type due to its focus on inventory, which most errand services don't have.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
Wave: Free for accounting and invoicing. Charges 2.9% + 30c per credit card payment. No inventory features. Limited reports. Best for: Solo errand runners, senior care providers, or TaskRabbit operators just starting out, tracking gas receipts and direct client payments.

FreshBooks: Starts at $19/month (Lite). Excellent for invoicing, tracking time, and giving clients a place to see their invoices. Best for: Concierge services or personal assistants with many hourly clients, needing professional invoices and easy online payments.

QuickBooks Online: Starts at $35/month (Simple Start). Has the most features, including payroll add-ons and many integrations. Can be harder to learn. Best for: Growing concierge businesses planning to hire other errand runners or a part-time scheduler, or those needing detailed financial reports.

Xero: Starts at $15/month (Early, limited). Allows unlimited users. Strong for inventory and purchase orders. Not usually a good fit for personal errand or senior companion services, as these businesses rarely manage inventory or need many users.

sections.heading: When to Choose Wave
sections.body: Choose Wave if you're just starting your personal errand service or senior companion business and have a few clients. It's free and helps you track simple income from direct deposits and expenses like gas, parking fees, or client-specific grocery purchases. It's perfect if you want clean books without a monthly bill while you test your business idea, like 'local pickup & delivery' or 'elderly assistance,' before committing to paid software. It works best if you have no plans to hire W2 employees.

sections.heading: When to Choose FreshBooks
sections.body: Choose FreshBooks if you charge clients by the hour for companion services or by project for personal shopping. You likely send many invoices for varied tasks (e.g., '3 hours senior companion,' 'grocery shopping & delivery flat fee'). It makes it easy for clients to pay online for tasks like post office runs, pet sitting, or appointment transport. You can also track your time spent driving, waiting, or completing tasks for accurate billing. The client portal can help you share task updates.

sections.heading: When to Choose QuickBooks Online
sections.body: Choose QuickBooks Online if you plan to expand your service and hire a part-time assistant for scheduling or another errand runner (even as a 1099 contractor). If your tax accountant already uses QuickBooks, it makes tax time much easier. You'll want detailed reports to see which services (e.g., dog walking, grocery delivery, senior care) are most profitable. It's also good if you mix services with selling small curated items (like gift baskets). You'll need it if you're looking for a small business loan to grow, like buying a new vehicle or expanding marketing.

sections.heading: When to Choose Xero
sections.body: Xero is generally not the best fit for most personal errand or senior companion services. Its strengths are in managing physical inventory and handling multiple currencies for international business. A typical errand runner doesn't carry inventory like a product business. While it allows unlimited users, most solo or small concierge teams won't need that feature to start. It could be an option for a very high-end concierge service with many international clients, but this is rare.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body: For most new personal errand runners or senior companions, start with Wave to keep costs down and easily track basic income and expenses like gas and supplies. If you are a personal shopper or concierge service that bills many hourly projects and sends frequent client invoices, FreshBooks is likely a better fit from day one. Move to QuickBooks Online when you hire help (even a part-time scheduler) or need more detailed financial reports to grow your business. Xero is rarely the best choice for this type of business unless you have very specific, niche needs.

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
Wave: Create a free account at waveapps.com, connect your business bank account, and categorize your first few client payments and expense receipts (like gas or grocery purchases for clients) to establish a baseline.

FreshBooks: Start the 30-day free trial. Use it to build a few client profiles and send your first test invoice for a 'personal shopping trip' or 'hourly senior companion visit.' This lets you see how it works with your actual services.

QuickBooks Online: Use the 30-day free trial. If you plan to hire an accountant for your errand business taxes, ask them which QuickBooks Online plan they suggest – they often have partner discounts.

Xero: The Early plan ($15/month) limits you to 20 invoices per month. For a concierge service doing many jobs each month, you'll hit that limit fast and will need to upgrade to the more expensive Growing plan ($42/month), so budget for that early on.

faqs.question: Can I switch accounting software later without losing my data?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is painful. Most platforms let you export a chart of accounts and transaction history as CSV. Plan a migration at a fiscal year-end to keep your books clean. Budget 4-8 hours for a clean migration plus CPA review time.

faqs.question: Do I need an accountant if I use accounting software?
faqs.answer: Software handles transaction recording and basic reporting. An accountant handles tax strategy, entity structure, and year-end filing. Most small businesses need both — software daily, accountant quarterly or annually.

faqs.question: Is Wave really free?
faqs.answer: The accounting, invoicing, and receipt scanning features are free. Wave charges transaction fees when clients pay invoices online (2.9% + 30c for credit cards, 1% for ACH). Payroll is an add-on starting at $20/month.

title: Bookkeeping for Personal Errands & Concierge Services: Bench vs QuickBooks vs Pilot
metaTitle: Best Bookkeeping for Personal Concierge & Errand Services: Bench vs QBO vs Pilot
metaDescription: Running a personal errand or concierge service means your time is money. Decide if Bench, QuickBooks, or Pilot is the best bookkeeping solution to track mileage, client payments, and expenses for your small service business.
primaryKeyword: bookkeeping for personal concierge service
secondaryKeywords: small business bookkeeping software for service providers
best accounting software for personal shoppers
intro: As an errand runner, personal shopper, or senior companion, your time is your most valuable asset. The big question isn't which bookkeeping tool is the fanciest — it's whether you should spend your valuable time tracking gas receipts and client payments, or if someone else should do it. Bench and Pilot offer full-service help, giving you back hours each week. QuickBooks gives you the tools to do it yourself, often saving money but costing time. Let's figure out what's right for your personal concierge service.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: Bench is best for personal errand services or senior companion providers who want clean monthly expense reports without logging into accounting software themselves. Pilot is overkill; it's for big tech startups, not solo concierge operators. QuickBooks is a good fit if you're tracking your own mileage and client payments, or if you plan to hire a local bookkeeper to help you with tax season.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
Bench: Bench starts around $299/month. You get a real bookkeeper who learns your personal errand business. They track your gas money, mileage, and client payments. It's usually cash-basis, which is perfect for most local service providers. You get monthly reports that show how much you earned and spent. No need to learn new software.

Pilot: Pilot starts at $499/month, but honestly, it's too much for almost every personal concierge service. It's built for big tech companies with investors and complex payroll, not for tracking your TaskRabbit earnings or senior care visits. This service is not what a small service provider needs.

QuickBooks Online: QuickBooks Online plans range from about $30 to $200+ per month for the software alone. You or your hired helper enter all your transactions. It's super flexible. You can connect it to your bank, credit cards, and payment apps like Stripe or Square for your client payments. Most CPAs know QuickBooks, which helps with tax time.

sections.heading: When to Choose Bench
sections.body: Choose Bench if you're an errand runner, personal shopper, or senior companion service bringing in under $100K a year (or even up to $1M). You want to spend zero time on bookkeeping. You just want monthly reports showing your gas expenses, supply purchases, and how much you made from each client. You don't have big investors asking for complex reports; simple cash-basis accounting is fine for your taxes.

sections.heading: When to Choose Pilot
sections.body: Pilot is not for personal errand or concierge services. It's designed for businesses with venture capital funding, complex employee stock options, or millions in delayed payments. If you're wondering if you need Pilot, you probably don't. Stick with options built for small service businesses.

sections.heading: When to Choose QuickBooks (DIY or with a Bookkeeper)
sections.body: Choose QuickBooks if you want to track your own expenses like gas, parking, and client supply costs. This is often the case if you're just starting your personal errand business or doing TaskRabbit jobs independently. It's also great if you plan to hire a local bookkeeper or CPA for a few hours each month or quarter to do the data entry and reconcile your accounts. You're watching every dollar and don't want to pay $300 a month for full-service bookkeeping just yet.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body:
Here's what we suggest for your personal errand or concierge service:

*   **Just starting or under $5K/month in earnings (e.g., part-time TaskRabbit, a few regular clients):** QuickBooks Online Simple Start or Wave (free software) if you do the books yourself.
*   **Growing, profitable, earning $5K-$10K+/month, and hate accounting:** Bench is worth the cost to save your time.
*   **For almost all personal service businesses:** Pilot is too much. Save your money.
The cost difference isn't just about service; it's about the type of business. Personal concierge services generally need simpler, cost-effective solutions.

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
Bench: For Bench, start their free trial. Link your business bank account and credit cards. A bookkeeper will be assigned quickly, usually within a day or two, and they'll help get your first month of spending and earnings sorted within a couple of weeks.

Pilot: If, against advice, you still think Pilot is for you, expect to schedule a deep-dive call. Their team will review your business setup and then spend 2-4 weeks setting up your accounting. Budget for extra fees if your financial records are messy. For most personal service businesses, this step is unnecessary.

QuickBooks: If you're doing it yourself, sign up for QuickBooks Online Simple Start. Use the free trial. Connect your bank account and any credit cards. Spend time categorizing your last few months of transactions (like gas, parking fees, client payments, app fees) to see if you like using the software. This lets you decide before you pay.

affiliates.name: Bench
affiliates.description: Managed bookkeeping from $299/month

affiliates.name: Pilot
affiliates.description: Startup-focused bookkeeping from $499/month

affiliates.name: QuickBooks Online
affiliates.description: 30-day free trial, then from $35/month

faqs.question: Does Bench use QuickBooks?
faqs.answer: No. Bench uses its own proprietary platform. This means you cannot export your data directly into QuickBooks if you switch. Plan for a migration project if you outgrow Bench.

faqs.question: Is Pilot worth the price for an early-stage startup?
faqs.answer: If you have raised a seed round, yes. Investor reporting, accrual accounting, and audit-readiness are worth more than $500/month when you are managing a round. Pre-seed, the price is hard to justify.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cash-basis and accrual accounting?
faqs.answer: Cash-basis records income when cash is received and expenses when paid. Accrual records income when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of when cash moves. Most businesses under $25M in revenue can use either, but investors and lenders generally prefer accrual.

title: Stripe vs PayPal vs Square: Best Payment Processing for Your Errand & Concierge Service
metaTitle: Errand Service Payments: Stripe, PayPal, Square Comparison
metaDescription: Choosing the right payment processor for your personal errand or concierge business is key. Compare Stripe, PayPal, and Square for mobile payments, recurring billing, and client convenience.
primaryKeyword: payment processing personal errand services
secondaryKeywords: how to accept payments errand runner
best payment gateway for service businesses
intro: Payment processing for your personal errand or concierge service feels simple until you dive into the details. Fees, how long it takes to get paid, and what happens when a client questions a charge can make a big difference. Stripe, PayPal, and Square each have strengths for different parts of your business, from accepting payments on the go to setting up recurring bills. Choosing the wrong one can cost you more than just the transaction fees.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: Stripe is often best if you plan to offer recurring services like senior companionship or regular personal shopping, or if you primarily book and invoice clients online. Square is built for accepting payments face-to-face, perfect for errand runners who need to swipe a card at a client's door or at the checkout counter. PayPal works well if your clients specifically ask for it, especially older demographics, or if you need to send a quick payment link without much setup.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
Stripe: Charges 2.9% + 30c per successful online transaction (like an invoice paid through a link). In-person is 2.7% + 5c using their reader. There's no monthly fee for standard use. It's great for setting up monthly subscriptions for ongoing clients, sending professional invoices, and managing payment links for pre-booked tasks or personal shopping lists.

PayPal: Charges 3.49% + 49c for standard online checkout (e.g., a PayPal button on your site). In-person via PayPal Zettle is 2.29% + 9c. Many clients, especially older ones, recognize and trust PayPal. It's easy to send quick payment requests via email or text. However, they are known for sometimes holding funds, which can slow down how fast you get paid.

Square: Charges 2.6% + 10c for in-person transactions. Online payments are 2.9% + 30c. You can often get a free basic card reader when you sign up, which is perfect for swiping cards on your phone or tablet at a client's location. Its simple app can help you track services provided, accept tips easily, and manage client details, even if you don't have 'inventory' in the traditional sense.

sections.heading: When to Choose Stripe
sections.body: Choose Stripe if you plan to manage many recurring clients for services like weekly errands or ongoing senior companionship. It's excellent for setting up automatic monthly billing. It's also ideal if you want to send professional invoices or secure payment links for pre-booked personal shopping jobs or one-off tasks. If you're building a website for clients to book and pay directly, Stripe's tools are very strong for that, helping you manage various payment flows and protect against fraud for higher-value services.

sections.heading: When to Choose Square
sections.body: Square is your best bet if you primarily accept payments directly from clients during or after an errand or service is completed. Think swiping a card at their home after a grocery delivery, or when you complete a task on location. The free card reader is a huge plus for mobile service providers, turning your phone or tablet into a reliable payment terminal. Its simple app allows you to quickly input a service cost, add a tip, and get paid on the spot, making it very straightforward for daily operations.

sections.heading: When to Choose PayPal
sections.body: Consider PayPal if a good number of your clients are older and specifically ask to pay with PayPal because they already have an account and trust it. It's also a quick option if you need to accept payment for a single errand or task right away, as you can send a payment request link via text or email with almost no setup. If you're starting out and just need a quick way to get paid without any complex systems, PayPal can get you accepting money in minutes.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body: For most Personal Errands & Concierge Services, if you're focusing on organized online booking, invoicing, and recurring client services, start with Stripe. If your business relies heavily on accepting payments on the spot at various locations, Square's mobile card readers and user-friendly app are likely a better fit. Only add PayPal as an additional option if you find clients specifically asking for it, or for quick, informal payment requests. Be aware of PayPal's reputation for account holds, especially as your business grows.

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
Stripe: Create an account at stripe.com, verify your business details, and you can start sending payment links or invoices to accept payments for your errand services the same day.

Square: Sign up at squareup.com, order their free card reader, and download the Square POS app. You'll be ready for in-person payments within 24-48 hours of your hardware arriving.

PayPal: Create a business account at paypal.com/business. You can immediately send payment requests or add a PayPal Checkout button to your website if you have one.

affiliates.name: Stripe
affiliates.description: Online payment processing with industry-leading API

affiliates.name: Square
affiliates.description: In-person POS + online payments with free hardware

affiliates.name: PayPal Business
affiliates.description: Global payments accepted by 400M+ consumers

faqs.question: Can I use Stripe and PayPal together?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many businesses use Stripe as the primary processor and add PayPal as a secondary option at checkout. This adds 5-15% additional conversion for customers who prefer PayPal. The trade-off is two separate payout schedules and two reconciliation streams.

faqs.question: Why do PayPal accounts get held?
faqs.answer: PayPal holds funds when their fraud algorithms flag unusual activity — a sudden spike in volume, high-value transactions, or a spike in disputes. Holds can last 180 days in extreme cases. Stripe and Square also have hold policies, but they are generally less aggressive and more transparent about resolution.

faqs.question: What are interchange fees and do I pay them?
faqs.answer: Interchange is the fee the card network charges the payment processor. With flat-rate pricing, you pay the listed rate and the processor absorbs variance. With interchange-plus pricing (available at higher volumes), you pay interchange directly plus a small markup — cheaper at scale.

title: Best Business Bank Account for Personal Errands & Concierge Services: Mercury, Relay, or Brex?
metaTitle: Business Bank Accounts for Errand Runners & Concierge Services | Mercury, Relay, Brex Comparison
metaDescription: Choosing a business bank for your personal errand service, concierge business, or senior companion startup? Compare Mercury, Relay, and Brex for solo operators, managing client funds, and separating expenses.
primaryKeyword: best business bank account personal errand service
intro: For personal errand runners, concierge professionals, or senior companions, your business bank account is vital. It's where you manage client payments, track expenses like gas and supplies, and keep your business money separate from personal funds. We look at Mercury, Brex Cash, and Relay – three popular online bank options – and compare how they help solo operators and small teams manage their unique money needs.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: For most independent errand runners or new concierge services, Mercury is the top pick: it’s simple, has no monthly fees, and is easy to use for managing client payments and tracking basic costs like fuel and supplies. Relay is better if you're growing, hiring an assistant, or want to keep client funds, owner pay, and specific operating costs (like vehicle maintenance or software subscriptions) in separate 'buckets' for easier tracking. Brex Cash only makes sense if you’re already using their corporate credit card for significant client purchases or operational spending and want everything in one place, which is less common for most personal errand businesses.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
Mercury: This bank has no monthly fees and no minimum balance. Your money is FDIC insured up to $5M. It offers a 'Treasury' product that can earn interest on money you're not using right away. Its connection tools (API) can be useful for linking to expense tracking apps or invoicing software. It handles standard payments like receiving client ACH transfers and sending checks.

Relay: Also has no monthly fees (Relay Pro costs $30/month for extra features). The key benefit here is up to 20 separate checking accounts and 50 debit cards for your business. This is fantastic for keeping client funds separate, setting aside money for specific client projects, or earmarking funds for gas, marketing, or your own salary. Its team controls are useful if you hire a part-time helper.

Brex Cash: This account is free if you use the Brex corporate credit card. Your funds are FDIC insured up to $6M. It offers a good yield on your operating cash. It's tightly connected to Brex's expense management system. You cannot open Brex Cash as a standalone bank account without also using their credit card.

sections.heading: When to Choose Mercury
sections.body: Choose Mercury if you are a solo personal errand runner, an independent TaskRabbit operator going solo, or starting a new concierge service. It’s perfect if you need a clean, no-fee business account that works right away to separate client payments and business expenses (like gas, cleaning supplies, or event tickets) from your personal money. You'll appreciate the fast setup process – most people get approved quickly. The account's connection tools (API) can also be helpful for automatically linking to budgeting tools or mileage trackers to make logging expenses easier.

sections.heading: When to Choose Relay
sections.body: Choose Relay if your concierge business is growing and you plan to hire a part-time assistant or driver, and you need to give them a debit card for specific purchases (e.g., client groceries, event tickets) with spending limits. It's also ideal if you want to separate your money for different purposes: a 'Gas & Vehicle Maintenance' account, a 'Marketing & Software Subscriptions' account, a 'Client Funds' account, and a 'My Pay' account. This makes tracking finances for taxes much simpler. If you manage funds for multiple clients, you can easily see how much belongs to each project or client. It helps you manage your money like the 'Profit First' system, ensuring you pay yourself and set aside funds for taxes and growth.

sections.heading: When to Choose Brex Cash
sections.body: Choose Brex Cash if your personal errand or concierge service has grown significantly and you're regularly making large purchases for clients (e.g., high-end gifts, event planning supplies) that require a corporate credit card. This account is best if you are already using the Brex card for these big purchases and want one platform to manage all your spending and cash. For most solo errand runners or small concierge services, Brex Cash isn't the first choice, as their typical spending patterns usually don't require a full-fledged corporate card system designed for rapid-growth businesses.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body: For most solo errand runners or new concierge businesses, start with Mercury. It's straightforward, free, and handles essential banking without any hidden catches. If your business grows, or if you find yourself needing to keep funds strictly separate – for specific client projects, gas money, or your own salary – then consider switching to or adding Relay. Its multiple account feature is excellent for managing different money buckets. Brex Cash is generally not the initial choice for this industry unless you have substantial business spending that already makes using the Brex corporate credit card a good fit for your operations.

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
Mercury: Apply at mercury.com. You'll need your business formation documents (like LLC papers or your Sole Proprietor info if applicable), your EIN (if you have one), and a personal ID. Approval is usually very quick, often within 24-48 hours, meaning you can start receiving client payments sooner.

Relay: Apply at relayfi.com. Before you start the application, take some time to plan how you want to split your business money. For example, think about separate accounts for 'Client Funds,' 'Operating Expenses (like Gas, Software Subscriptions),' 'Owner's Pay,' and 'Tax Savings.' Setting up your desired account structure early will save you time and effort later.

Brex Cash: This account is only available when you apply for the Brex corporate credit card. This means it's typically considered later, once your errand or concierge business has grown to a point where it has significant recurring expenses that justify signing up for a corporate card program.

affiliates.name: Mercury
affiliates.description: No-fee startup banking with API access

affiliates.name: Relay
affiliates.description: Multi-account business banking for teams

affiliates.name: Brex
affiliates.description: Banking + corporate cards in one platform

faqs.question: Are Mercury and Relay real banks?
faqs.answer: No. Both are financial technology companies that partner with FDIC-insured banks to hold deposits. Your deposits are FDIC insured, but Mercury and Relay themselves are not chartered banks.

faqs.question: Can I use Mercury or Relay for payroll?
faqs.answer: Yes — both support ACH transfers needed for payroll. But most businesses use a dedicated payroll platform (Gusto, Rippling, ADP) and simply connect it to their Mercury or Relay account for the ACH pull.

faqs.question: Which account earns the most interest on cash?
faqs.answer: Mercury Treasury earns a competitive yield on balances over $500K. For smaller balances, Brex Cash currently offers the highest yield on operating balances among the three. Rates change frequently, so compare current rates at account opening.

title: Choosing the Best Business Expense Card for Personal Errands & Concierge Services
metaTitle: Best Expense Cards for Errand Runners & Concierge Services: Brex, Ramp, Divvy
metaDescription: Running a personal errand or concierge service? Find the best business expense card for gas, client purchases, and supplies. Compare Brex, Ramp, and Divvy for your errand running or personal shopping business. Get spend control and easy receipt capture.
primaryKeyword: best business expense card personal errands
intro: As an errand runner, personal shopper, or concierge service, keeping track of client purchases, gas, and other business costs is key. Business expense cards are no longer just for big companies. Brex, Ramp, and Divvy offer cards that help you manage spending, capture receipts, and link to your accounting software. They each have different rules for who gets a card, how much credit you get, and what rewards you earn.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: For most independent errand runners and personal shoppers, **Ramp** stands out. It's great for tracking every dollar spent on client items, gas, and supplies, helping you save money with its automated system and no monthly fees. **Divvy (now BILL Spend and Expense)** is a good option if you’re just starting out or need a credit line to cover big client purchases before they pay you back. You don’t need a huge bank balance to qualify. **Brex** is generally for larger, well-funded businesses, so it's less likely to be the best fit for most personal concierge services unless you have significant cash flow.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
Ramp: Free to use. It's a charge card, meaning you pay off your full balance each month. Your credit limit will depend on how much cash you keep in your business bank account, usually 50-75% of that amount. This means if you keep $5,000 for operations, your limit could be around $2,500-$3,750 – enough for most client shopping trips or bulk supply buys. You get 1.5% cash back on all spending, like gas, client groceries, or monthly software. It automatically matches receipts and links to your accounting software like QuickBooks Self-Employed or Wave. No personal guarantee needed.

Brex: Free for smaller operations, with premium features costing extra if you add more team members later. It's also a charge card, paid in full each month. Brex usually gives high credit limits, but these are mostly for businesses with significant funding or a lot of cash flow. This might not be suitable for most independent errand runners. Rewards are often tiered, meaning you get more points for travel or certain software, which might not align with typical errand service expenses like gas or client purchases. A personal guarantee is usually not needed if your business is very well-funded.

Divvy (BILL Spend and Expense): Free to use. This card offers a revolving credit line, meaning you can carry a balance over to the next month, which can be helpful if a client payment is delayed. Your credit limit is based on Divvy's review of your business, making it easier for new or bootstrapped errand services to qualify without needing huge amounts of cash in the bank. You can earn rewards, often by paying off your balance weekly instead of monthly – a good rhythm for managing daily errand costs. A personal guarantee might be asked for, especially when you're just starting out.

sections.heading: When to Choose Ramp
sections.body: Choose Ramp if: You regularly keep a decent amount of cash in your business bank account (e.g., $5,000-$20,000) and are okay with your credit limit being based on that. You want to fully automate tracking all your errand expenses — from gas fill-ups and client-requested specific items to cleaning supplies. Ramp’s system is excellent for matching receipts for every coffee run or grocery stop, making tax time easier. You want to save money through good tracking rather than chasing complicated rewards points. You use popular accounting software like QuickBooks Self-Employed, Wave, or Xero for your errand business.

sections.heading: When to Choose Brex
sections.body: Brex is a fit if: Your personal errands or concierge business has significant funding, perhaps from a larger investment, and needs a very high credit limit. You frequently fly for special concierge jobs or use ride-share services often for business travel and want rewards specifically for those costs. You want a card that looks impressive when dealing with high-end vendors or suppliers, or if you plan to scale up to a larger operation quickly. For most independent errand runners, Brex will likely offer more than what's needed.

sections.heading: When to Choose Divvy
sections.body: Divvy is a good choice if: You run your errand or concierge service on your own, without big investor money, but still need a reliable credit line. You might need to cover a large client purchase ($500+ for groceries, event supplies) before you get paid, and want the option to pay it back over a few weeks. You are comfortable with BILL's other business tools. You can make weekly payments to earn the best rewards, which helps manage expenses for your daily tasks.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body: For most independent errand runners or personal concierge services, **Ramp** is the top choice for its easy expense tracking and focus on saving money on everyday purchases like gas and client supplies. If you need a credit line to cover client costs before payments come in, and you're running on a tighter budget, **Divvy** is a strong contender. Brex is usually for much larger, well-funded businesses, not typically a good fit for most individual errand services. All three platforms offer their core expense tracking for free; the main differences are how they decide your credit limit, what rewards you get, and how often you need to pay back what you've spent.

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
Ramp: Apply online quickly, usually in under 10 minutes. You’ll connect your business bank account, and Ramp uses your current cash balance to set your initial credit limit for errands and supplies. You can get your first virtual cards to start spending in just 1-3 business days.

Brex: Apply through their website. If your concierge service has significant funding, have those details ready. Limits are set when you join and can grow as your business earns more or receives more cash.

Divvy: Apply through the BILL website. The review process for your business typically takes 1-3 days. Initial credit limits for new errand services might be lower than Ramp or Brex, but they can increase over time as you show good payment history.

affiliates.name: Ramp
affiliates.description: Free expense management + corporate cards

affiliates.name: Brex
affiliates.description: Corporate cards for startups and growth companies

affiliates.name: Divvy
affiliates.description: Business credit + expense management by BILL

faqs.question: Do Ramp and Brex require a personal guarantee?
faqs.answer: Generally no, for charge cards. Ramp and Brex use your business cash position or funding to underwrite limits without requiring a personal guarantee. Divvy may require one for newer businesses or lower credit profiles.

faqs.question: Can I use these alongside my existing bank account?
faqs.answer: Yes. None of these are banks (except Brex, which has its own cash management product). You keep your business bank account and use the card platform on top of it.

faqs.question: What happens to my Brex account if I run out of runway?
faqs.answer: Brex monitors cash position and can reduce limits if cash falls significantly. If you shut down, any outstanding balance is due immediately. Charge cards require full payoff and cannot be used as a bridge.

title: Gusto vs Rippling vs ADP: Best Payroll for Personal Errands & Concierge Services
metaTitle: Payroll Software for Errand Runners & Concierge Businesses | Gusto, Rippling, ADP
metaDescription: Choose the best payroll software for your personal errand service, senior companion business, or independent task operation. Compare Gusto, Rippling, and ADP for managing W-2 employees and 1099 contractors.
primaryKeyword: payroll software personal errand concierge services
intro: Payroll is a critical task for any personal errand or concierge business, from solo operators taking on occasional tasks to growing senior companion services. Getting it wrong means unhappy task runners or service providers. Gusto, Rippling, and ADP offer different approaches: simplicity for new businesses, platform integration for growing teams, and robust compliance for larger operations. The best choice depends on whether you primarily use 1099 contractors, W-2 employees, and how quickly your team of errand runners or companions is expanding.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: Gusto is the ideal starting point for most new personal errand services, solo operators, or small concierge businesses. It’s simple to use and handles both W-2 employees (like an office manager) and 1099 contractors (your task runners) without you needing to be an HR expert. Rippling is a better fit if you’re quickly growing your W-2 staff beyond 5-10 people or managing a large pool of 30+ contractors, needing to automate onboarding for things like background checks, assigning apps, and managing scheduling access. ADP is for more established operations with 25+ W-2 employees, especially if you have complex multi-state payroll, dedicated HR needs, or operate in a highly regulated niche like specialized elder care.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
Gusto: Simple plan starts at $40/month + $6/person/month. Offers full-service payroll, automatic tax filing for W-2s, and easy 1099 contractor payments and year-end forms. Includes employee/contractor self-service for pay stubs and tax forms. Handles basic benefits administration if you decide to offer them to core W-2 staff.

Rippling: Starts at $8/person/month (modular pricing). Provides a unified platform for payroll, benefits, HR, and IT (like assigning scheduling apps or mileage trackers). Excellent for automating onboarding workflows for new task runners or companions, including background checks. Has a steeper learning curve but offers powerful automation.

ADP Run (small business): Starts at $79/month + $4/person/month. Full-service payroll with robust tax compliance. Offers access to HR advisors, useful for navigating contractor vs. employee classifications or specific state regulations for care services. The user interface can feel a bit dated but is highly functional.

sections.heading: When to Choose Gusto
sections.body: Choose Gusto if you manage under 15-20 regular W-2 employees and a roster of 1099 contractors. It’s perfect when you want payroll for your task runners, personal shoppers, or administrative staff to just work without requiring an HR background. Gusto efficiently handles both hourly W-2 employees and 1099 contractors, making it easy to pay for tasks, mileage, or scheduled shifts. It also allows you to offer basic benefits (like health stipends or simple 401k) through the same platform for your core W-2 team, if desired.

sections.heading: When to Choose Rippling
sections.body: Opt for Rippling if you are rapidly scaling your W-2 team (growing from 5 to 15+ W-2 employees in 12 months) or managing a large network of 30+ independent contractors. It's ideal when you need to automate onboarding beyond just payroll – think setting up background checks for new senior companions, provisioning access to your secure scheduling and client communication apps, or assigning GPS tracking for your errand runners. If you need a single system to manage all team data, from HR to payment and app access, and have the budget for a premium platform, Rippling is a strong contender.

sections.heading: When to Choose ADP
sections.body: Consider ADP if your W-2 employee count exceeds 25-30, especially if your personal errand or concierge business operates in a niche with complex compliance (e.g., specialized medical transport, in-home care requiring specific state licensing). It's also suitable if you have W-2 employees across multiple states, need enterprise-grade reporting, or require dedicated HR support for navigating employment laws specific to caregivers or mobile service providers. If you acquire a competing service or merge with a team already using ADP, it can also be a logical choice.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body: For most personal errand and concierge services, especially those starting out or with primarily 1099 contractors, begin with Gusto. It handles both W-2s and 1099s simply and effectively for teams up to 15 W-2 employees plus a larger contractor pool. When your W-2 employee count hits 10-15, and you find yourself spending significant time on onboarding new task runners – managing background checks, app access, and training – evaluate Rippling for its automation capabilities. Consider ADP when your W-2 team crosses 25-30 employees, you enter a heavily regulated service area, or require a dedicated HR support relationship. The migration from Gusto to Rippling is relatively straightforward, allowing you to switch as your business grows.

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
Gusto: Sign up directly at gusto.com. Enter your business details, EIN, add your W-2 employees and 1099 contractors, and you can typically run your first payroll within a week. Gusto assists with state registration for payroll taxes across all 50 states.

Rippling: Request a demo from their website. Rippling usually requires a scoping call to understand your specific HR, IT, and payroll needs for your errand or concierge service before setup. Expect a 2-4 week implementation time if you're using multiple modules like IT provisioning and HR.

ADP Run: You can start with their online sign-up flow or request a sales call for customized pricing. ADP assigns an implementation specialist who can help migrate your existing payroll history and ensure a smooth transition, especially if you have a complex W-2 team.

affiliates.name: Gusto
affiliates.description: Full-service payroll from $40/month + $6/person

affiliates.name: Rippling
affiliates.description: Unified HR, payroll, and IT from $8/person/month

affiliates.name: ADP Run
affiliates.description: Enterprise payroll and HR compliance

faqs.question: Does payroll software file my payroll taxes automatically?
faqs.answer: Gusto, Rippling, and ADP all offer full-service payroll that calculates, files, and remits federal, state, and local payroll taxes automatically. Verify that auto-filing is included in your plan tier before you commit.

faqs.question: Can Gusto handle contractors (1099s) and employees (W-2s) together?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gusto supports both contractor and employee payroll in the same account. Contractors are paid via direct deposit and receive 1099-NEC forms at year-end.

faqs.question: How hard is it to switch payroll providers mid-year?
faqs.answer: Possible but annoying. You need to transfer year-to-date payroll totals so W-2s are accurate at year-end. The cleanest migration point is January 1st. Mid-year is doable if you have complete YTD records from the old provider.

title: Funding Your Personal Errands & Concierge Service: Bootstrap, Angel, or VC?
metaTitle: Personal Errands & Concierge Funding | Bootstrap Errand Service | Angel Investment Senior Companion
metaDescription: Discover the best funding path for your Personal Errands & Concierge Service. Learn when to bootstrap your errand business, seek angel investment for expansion, or avoid venture capital for your senior companion service.
primaryKeyword: funding personal errands & concierge services
intro: For your Personal Errands & Concierge Services business, deciding how to fund your launch is about what kind of business you want to build. Do you want to serve a local community, building relationships and keeping full control? Or do you dream of a large-scale operation, even if it means giving up a piece of your company? Understanding these trade-offs helps you pick the right funding path for your errand service, personal shopping business, or senior companion startup.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: Most Personal Errands & Concierge Services should bootstrap. You can reach profitability fast, often within weeks, using your own car, phone, and local network. This keeps you in charge of your client list, services, and growth pace. Consider angel investment only if you plan to quickly expand beyond a solo operation, perhaps buying a branded vehicle, hiring a few runners, or developing a simple client portal. You might need $20K-$100K for this specific growth, not millions. Venture capital is almost never suitable for a local Personal Errands & Concierge Service. Their model demands massive, rapid growth and a 10x return, which doesn't fit a business built on personal service and local trust.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
Bootstrapping: 0% dilution. Full control over your routes, clients, and service offerings. Constrained by your personal time and local client demand. Requires you to cover fuel, insurance, and phone costs from your first paying clients.

Angel Investment: Typically $20K-$100K from local investors. You might give up 5-15% of your company for this. This money could help you hire a part-time assistant, buy professional dispatch software ($50-$150/month), or run targeted local social media ads. Angels usually understand the slower, steady growth of a service business.

Venture Capital: This path is designed for tech startups aiming for global scale and billion-dollar exits. For a local personal service business, seeking VC funding is usually a mismatch. You would face demands for growth that would fundamentally change the nature of your personalized service.

sections.heading: When to Bootstrap
sections.body: Your business offers direct, personal services like grocery delivery, senior check-ins, or managing home appointments. These services thrive on trust and local relationships, not national scale. You can start with minimal costs: just your reliable vehicle, a smartphone for scheduling, liability insurance ($500-$1500/year), and business registration fees ($50-$300). Many personal concierge services become profitable within the first month by charging $30-$75 per hour and leveraging their personal network for initial clients. This path lets you set your own hours and choose your clients without outside pressure.

sections.heading: When to Raise Angel Investment
sections.body: You've proven your local errand service model and want to expand within your city, but your personal savings won't cover it. Maybe you want to hire your first 2-3 dedicated errand runners, invest in a professional, branded vehicle wrap ($1,500-$3,000), or subscribe to advanced scheduling and dispatch software ($50-$200/month per user). Angels could provide the $20,000-$100,000 needed to scale from a solo operation to a small, professional team, allowing you to serve more clients and build a stronger local brand. They might offer advice on efficient routing or local marketing strategies.

sections.heading: When to Raise Venture Capital
sections.body: This funding path is almost never right for a traditional Personal Errands & Concierge Service. VC is for businesses that can become a nationwide, technology-driven platform like Uber or Instacart, where the first company to achieve massive scale wins. A local business built on personal trust and direct service doesn't fit this model. If your vision is to build a tech company that enables thousands of independent errand runners across the country, then you're building a different kind of business altogether, and VC might be relevant for that tech platform, not the direct service itself.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body: For a Personal Errands & Concierge Services business, success often means building a strong local reputation, serving a loyal client base, and achieving a comfortable profit margin for yourself and a small team. This means making $100K-$500K in annual revenue is a great outcome. Venture capital aims for billion-dollar companies. Trying to fit your personal service business into a VC model is like trying to put a square peg in a round hole; it forces you to prioritize growth above all else, which often means sacrificing the personal touch that makes your business special. Stick to bootstrapping or, at most, seeking a small angel investment for specific, controlled local expansion.

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
Bootstrapping: Map out your first 3-6 months. Figure out your 'ramen profitability' for personal errand running: how many hours at what rate do you need to cover your fuel, insurance, phone bill, and personal living expenses? Identify your initial target clients (e.g., busy professionals, seniors). Set up a simple online booking link or phone number.

Angel Investment: If you aim to expand, start by proving your service with paying clients first. Then, create a simple business plan showing how angel money (e.g., $50,000) will allow you to hire two part-time runners, invest in a basic CRM, and boost local online ads. Connect with local business owners or successful entrepreneurs in your community; they often act as angels for businesses they understand.

Venture Capital: This step is generally not applicable. Focus your energy on building a solid, profitable service business instead.

affiliates.name: AngelList
affiliates.description: Connect with angel investors and launch a fundraise

affiliates.name: Capchase
affiliates.description: Non-dilutive capital for SaaS businesses

faqs.question: What is a SAFE and how does it work?
faqs.answer: A SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity) is a contract where an investor gives you money today in exchange for the right to receive equity in a future priced round at a discount or with a valuation cap. SAFEs are not debt — they do not accrue interest or have a maturity date.

faqs.question: How much equity should I give up in a seed round?
faqs.answer: The standard is 10-20% for a seed round of $500K-$3M. Below 10% dilution per round is typical for founders with strong leverage. Above 25% dilution in a single round should prompt a closer look at valuation expectations.

faqs.question: Can I raise angel money and stay bootstrapped?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many founders raise a small angel round ($100K-$500K) to buy time to reach profitability without committing to the VC growth path. As long as your SAFEs have no board seats or control provisions, angel money can be taken without giving up operational independence.

title: Funding Your Personal Errands & Concierge Business: SBA Loans, Lines of Credit, & RBF Compared
metaTitle: Best Loans for Personal Errands & Concierge Services | SBA vs. LOC vs. RBF
metaDescription: Discover the right financing for your Personal Errands & Concierge business. Compare SBA loans, business lines of credit, and revenue-based financing to fund vehicles, software, or expansion for your errand service or senior companion business.
primaryKeyword: financing personal errands & concierge services
secondaryKeywords: business line of credit for personal shopper
capital for TaskRabbit operators going independent
small business loans for concierge services
intro: Funding your Personal Errands & Concierge Service isn't a one-size-fits-all problem. Whether you're an errand runner, a personal shopper, breaking free from TaskRabbit, or starting a senior companion service, the type of debt you choose matters. An SBA loan, a business line of credit, and revenue-based financing each solve different problems at different costs, with different hoops to jump through. Picking the wrong one costs you more than just extra interest – it can tie your hands when you need flexibility most, like when your new service vehicle needs unexpected repairs or you want to expand your client roster.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: SBA loans offer the lowest interest rates and longest repayment times, perfect if you're buying a dedicated service vehicle or expanding to a small administrative hub, but expect a 30-90 day wait and proof of at least two years in business with good credit. A business line of credit is your best friend for managing uneven cash flow—think covering gas price spikes, software subscription renewals for your client management system, or a sudden marketing push. You only pay interest on what you use, making it ideal for the unpredictable nature of client bookings. Revenue-based financing (RBF) is the fastest option for established errand services with steady monthly retainer clients who need quick capital for growth, like a targeted ad campaign for senior companion services, without giving up a piece of your business or needing physical collateral.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
SBA 7(a) Loan: You can borrow up to $5 million. Interest rates are usually prime + 2.25-4.75% (currently around 10-12%). Repay over 10-25 years. You'll need at least two years in business, good personal credit (680+), and collateral for loans over $25K (like a vehicle title or property). Expect approval in 30-90 days.

Business Line of Credit: Typically $10K-$500K. Interest rates usually range from 7-25%+, depending on who lends you the money. It's revolving—borrow, pay back, borrow again. You'll likely need at least one year in business and about $50K+ in annual client billings. Online lenders can approve these in 1-7 days.

Revenue-Based Financing: Amounts from $10K-$5 million. No interest rate; instead, you pay a fixed fee (called a capital factor, usually 1.1x-1.5x the amount borrowed). You repay this as a percentage of your monthly revenue, typically 5-20%. You'll need $10K+/month in consistent revenue from client retainers or regular bookings and at least six months in business. Approval can be as fast as 24-72 hours.

sections.heading: When to Choose an SBA Loan
sections.body: Pick an SBA loan if you need a lot of money (over $100K) for a major move, like buying a dedicated, reliable fuel-efficient vehicle (e.g., a minivan or large sedan ideal for packages and senior transport) or acquiring a competitor's established client list and service route. You'll get the lowest possible interest rate but must be ready to wait 60-90 days for the money. This option is best if your errand or concierge business has been operating for over two years, you have good personal credit, and you have assets that can serve as collateral, such as a business vehicle or personal property.

sections.heading: When to Choose a Business Line of Credit
sections.body: A line of credit is your financial safety net, not a big lump sum for a single purchase. It's perfect for managing the ups and downs of client demand or unexpected costs. For instance, if gas prices suddenly jump, your scheduling software subscription is due, or you need to hire temporary help for holiday personal shopping rushes. It's also great for bridging gaps when clients pay on different schedules. You only pay interest on the money you actually use, and you can draw and repay as needed. This flexibility makes it the smart choice for most personal errand and concierge businesses to cover working capital needs.

sections.heading: When to Choose Revenue-Based Financing
sections.body: Consider RBF if your concierge or errand service has consistent monthly income, perhaps from ongoing senior companion contracts or subscription-based personal shopping clients, and you need money fast—like within 48-72 hours. This is ideal for quickly funding a targeted digital ad campaign to expand your service area, investing in a new CRM system for better client management, or purchasing new specialized equipment without giving up equity. RBF is more expensive than a traditional bank loan but often cheaper than selling a part of your growing business. Only use RBF to fund activities that directly bring in more client bookings and revenue, not to cover operating losses.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body: For the absolute cheapest money, an SBA loan is the winner, but only if your errand or concierge service is established (2+ years) and you can wait. The most flexible option is a business line of credit; it's smart to set one up when your business is doing well, not when you're desperate for cash to cover a unexpected vehicle repair. RBF is the quickest and most friendly for owner-operators with steady client revenue, especially for growth initiatives like marketing, but remember its total cost is higher than bank debt. Always match the funding to your need: a large purchase for an established business (SBA), fluctuating operational costs (Line of Credit), or rapid growth for a revenue-generating service (RBF).

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
SBA Loan: Visit sba.gov/lender-match to find banks approved for SBA loans. Gather your last two years of business and personal tax returns, detailed profit & loss statements, balance sheets, and any business contracts or service agreements to show stability.

Line of Credit: Start with your current business bank. Also check online lenders like BlueVine, Fundbox, or OnDeck for faster approvals, though rates might be higher. The key is to apply when your personal errand business is healthy and growing, not when you're in a financial crunch.

Revenue-Based Financing: Look into Clearco or Capchase. You'll typically connect your bank accounts, client invoicing software (like QuickBooks), or payment processors to show consistent revenue. Offers can often be ready within 24 hours.

affiliates.name: BlueVine
affiliates.description: Business line of credit up to $250K

affiliates.name: Clearco
affiliates.description: Revenue-based financing for e-commerce and SaaS

affiliates.name: Capchase
affiliates.description: Non-dilutive growth capital for SaaS businesses

faqs.question: Does applying for a business loan hurt my personal credit?
faqs.answer: A hard inquiry occurs when a lender pulls your personal credit as part of a full application. Many online lenders do a soft pull for pre-qualification, which does not affect your score.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a term loan and a line of credit?
faqs.answer: A term loan gives you a lump sum upfront that you repay over a fixed schedule. A line of credit is revolving — you draw what you need, repay it, and borrow again up to your limit.

faqs.question: Is revenue-based financing considered debt or equity?
faqs.answer: Debt. RBF is a loan that you repay from future revenue. It does not involve giving up equity or ownership. However, most RBF providers use a revenue purchase agreement structure, which has different legal protections than a traditional loan.

title: LLC vs S-Corp vs C-Corp: Tax Strategies for Your Personal Errands & Concierge Service
metaTitle: Errand Service Business Structure: LLC, S-Corp, C-Corp Tax Savings
metaDescription: Discover the best business structure (LLC, S-Corp, C-Corp) for your personal errands, concierge, or senior companion service. Learn how to save on taxes as an independent tasker.
primaryKeyword: Personal Errands Concierge Business Structure Taxes
intro: You're busy running errands, shopping for clients, or providing companionship, and now it's time to choose the right legal structure for your business. The LLC, S-Corp, and C-Corp options aren't just legal choices; they're tax decisions that can save or cost you thousands each year. For a personal errands or concierge service, the best choice depends on your profit level, how you take money out, and your plans for growth.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: For most solo errand runners, personal shoppers, or new senior companion services, an LLC (taxed as a sole proprietor) is the right starting point. It’s simple, flexible, and avoids double taxation. Consider electing S-Corp status when your net profit from fetching groceries or assisting seniors consistently exceeds $50,000 per year — the self-employment tax savings become significant at that level. A C-Corp is almost never necessary for an independent errand or concierge business unless you plan to raise venture capital to build a large-scale, multi-state operation.

sections.heading: Side-by-Side Breakdown
sections.body:
An LLC (default, pass-through) means all net profit from your personal shopping or senior companion tasks is subject to self-employment tax (15.3% on the first $174,400 of net earnings in 2024). This income simply passes through to your personal tax return. It’s the simplest to maintain, requiring minimal paperwork beyond your regular tax filing, and there's no double taxation.

An LLC with S-Corp Election keeps the pass-through taxation. You'll pay yourself a reasonable salary (e.g., what you’d pay a full-time errand manager in your area), which is subject to payroll taxes. Any remaining profit you take as a distribution is NOT subject to the 15.3% self-employment tax. This saves you money, but adds costs like payroll setup, quarterly payroll filings, and higher CPA fees.

With a C-Corp, corporate income is first taxed at 21% (federal). Then, owners also pay tax on any dividends or salary they take – this is called double taxation. C-Corps are useful if you plan to issue multiple share classes for investors, retain earnings at the lower corporate rate, or if you ever aim for a massive scale requiring institutional investment. For a typical errand or concierge service, this structure is usually overkill and creates unnecessary tax burdens.

sections.heading: When to Stay an LLC
sections.body: Keep your personal errand or concierge business structured as an LLC if your net profit from driving, shopping, or assisting clients is under $50,000 per year. At this level, the potential S-Corp tax savings typically don't outweigh the added administrative costs and complexity. It's the simplest possible structure, perfect while you're validating your services, building your client base, and ensuring consistent income. If you're a solo errand runner or personal shopper without plans to bring on institutional investors, an LLC provides maximum flexibility in managing your finances and is easy to set up and maintain.

sections.heading: When to Elect S-Corp Status
sections.body: Consider electing S-Corp status when your personal errand or concierge business is consistently netting over $50,000 per year. If you're currently paying self-employment tax on all of that profit, an S-Corp election can significantly reduce your tax burden. The math: if your errand business earns $150,000 in profit (after deducting costs like gas, insurance, and supplies) and you pay yourself a reasonable salary of $80,000 (what a market-rate full-time errand manager would earn), you save 15.3% self-employment tax on the remaining $70,000 distribution — that's approximately $10,710 in annual tax savings. Make this move when your CPA confirms the numbers make sense for your specific income and expenses.

sections.heading: When to Form a C-Corp
sections.body: This is extremely rare for personal errand or concierge businesses. You should only consider a C-Corp if you plan to raise money from venture capital or institutional investors — VCs require C-Corps because they need preferred stock. It’s also required if you plan to offer qualified stock options to employees (ISOs require a C-Corp). For instance, if your vision is to build a national chain of AI-powered errand services, requiring millions in startup capital, a C-Corp would be necessary. This is not the structure for growing your local senior companion service or adding another personal shopping client.

sections.heading: The Verdict
sections.body: Start your personal errand service, personal shopping venture, or senior companion business as an LLC. It's straightforward, protects your personal assets, and simplifies your early tax filings. Make the S-Corp election when your CPA confirms the self-employment tax savings on your errand business profit exceed the additional compliance cost (typically at $50,000-$80,000 in net profit, depending on your state and specific expenses). Convert to a C-Corp only if you pursue venture capital funding. Do not form a C-Corp speculatively; the administrative overhead is significant, and the double-taxation problem is real if your business doesn't raise institutional capital and scale rapidly.

sections.heading: How to Get Started
sections.body:
For LLC formation: File Articles of Organization with your state's Secretary of State (costs vary from $50-$500 depending on the state). This crucial step protects your personal assets from business liabilities, like a delivery accident or a client dispute. Get an EIN from irs.gov (free, takes about 5 minutes online) and use it to open a dedicated business bank account. Keep your business and personal finances separate.

For S-Corp election: File IRS Form 2553 within 75 days of the start of the tax year you want it to apply. Before filing, have a CPA calculate a reasonable salary for yourself, considering what a similar errand manager or concierge professional would earn.

For C-Corp: If you absolutely need one, incorporate in Delaware (the standard for VC-backed companies). Tools like Stripe Atlas or Clerky can help, or you'll need a startup lawyer. This step is usually not relevant for personal service businesses.

affiliates.name: Stripe Atlas
affiliates.description: Delaware C-Corp formation in minutes

affiliates.name: Clerky
affiliates.description: Startup legal documents and incorporation

faqs.question: Can I switch from an LLC to an S-Corp later?
faqs.answer: Yes. An LLC can elect S-Corp tax treatment without changing its legal structure. File IRS Form 2553. The election must be made within 75 days of the tax year start.

faqs.question: What is a reasonable salary for S-Corp purposes?
faqs.answer: The IRS requires that S-Corp owner-employees pay themselves a salary comparable to what the position would pay in an arm's-length transaction. CPAs typically recommend 40-60% of total S-Corp profit as salary, with the remainder taken as distribution.

faqs.question: Does forming a Delaware C-Corp mean I pay Delaware taxes?
faqs.answer: Delaware has a franchise tax (minimum $175-$400/year for small companies). You do not pay Delaware income tax unless you have business operations or employees in Delaware.

title: Build a Financial Model for Your Personal Errands & Concierge Business: Plan for Profit
metaTitle: Financial Model for Personal Errands & Concierge Services | Profit Planning
metaDescription: Learn how to create a simple financial model for your personal errands, concierge, or senior companion service. Plan income, manage expenses, and ensure profitability for your new business.
primaryKeyword: personal errands business financial model
secondaryKeywords: how to price personal assistant services
intro: Starting a personal errands or concierge service business? Don't guess your finances. Most new service businesses over-estimate income and under-estimate costs like gas, liability insurance, and booking software. A strong financial model isn't just about predicting profit; it's your roadmap to understand what truly drives your income and expenses. It shows you what needs to happen for your errand running, personal shopping, or senior companion service to truly succeed.

sections.heading: The Quick Answer
sections.body: For your personal errands or concierge business, a solid financial model has three main parts: how you make money (based on tasks, hours, or clients, not just hopeful guesses), all your costs (including your own time and mileage), and a clear look at your cash flow. The rest is just how it looks on paper.

sections.heading: What 'Funders' Actually Look For
sections.body: Whether you're looking for a small loan from a bank or just making sense of your own business, the numbers need to add up. People checking your plan want to see that you understand every cost and income source. Does your growth plan match how many hours you can work or how many helpers you might hire? Do your costs for gas, liability insurance, and client management software line up with how busy you expect to be? Watch out if your income grows without showing how you'll get more clients or increase your service capacity. Also, don't show just one perfect income plan – always have a backup plan for when things are slow.

sections.heading: Revenue Model: Build From Drivers
sections.body:
